name: Nicaraguan Gallo Pinto
steps: Saute the onion in the oil until soft.
Add the black beans with a little of their liquid and the cooked rice.
Fry everything together, stirring often, until the rice takes on a light color from the beans.
steps_detailed: Cook the rice ahead of time (or use leftover rice - this dish is traditionally made with rice from the day before).
Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic in the vegetable oil over medium heat for 4-5 minutes until soft.
Add the black beans along with a few tablespoons of their liquid, and simmer 3-4 minutes.
Add the rice and fry everything together over medium-high heat, stirring and pressing frequently, for 6-8 minutes until the rice is evenly speckled and lightly crisp in spots.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Nicaraguan Indio Viejo
steps: Brown the ground beef with the onion until cooked through.
Whisk the masa harina into the diced tomatoes and a cup of water until smooth, then stir into the beef.
Simmer 10-12 minutes, stirring often, until thickened into a stew.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with the onion, bell pepper, and garlic over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes, until the meat is cooked through and the vegetables are soft.
Stir in the diced tomatoes, oregano, and cumin, and simmer 5 minutes.
In a separate bowl, whisk the masa harina into the beef broth until completely smooth with no lumps.
Pour the masa mixture into the pot, stirring constantly to prevent clumping, and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook, stirring often, for 12-15 minutes until the stew thickens to a hearty, spoonable consistency.

name: Nicaraguan Gallo Pinto with Eggs
steps: Saute the onion, then add the rice and beans and stir-fry until well combined.
Fry the egg separately.
Serve together.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in the oil until softened, then add the rice and beans, stir-frying 5-6 minutes until well combined and lightly toasted.
Fry the egg in a separate pan to your liking.
Top with cilantro and serve together - gallo pinto with a fried egg is a classic, hearty Nicaraguan breakfast.

name: Nicaraguan Fried Plantains with Cheese
steps: Fry the plantain slices until golden.
Serve with the crumbled queso fresco.
steps_detailed: Fry the plantain slices in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and caramelized.
Serve warm with the crumbled queso fresco - a common, comforting Nicaraguan breakfast pairing.

name: Nicaraguan Atol de Elote

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Blend the corn with the milk until smooth.
Simmer with the sugar for 8-10 minutes, stirring, until thickened.
Serve warm.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Blend the corn with the milk until very smooth.
Simmer with the sugar and cinnamon over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, 10-12 minutes until thick and warming.
Atol de elote, a sweet corn drink, is a common, comforting Nicaraguan breakfast.

name: Nicaraguan Quesillo

ingredients.note: oz, as a queso quesillo stand-in

steps: Warm the tortilla until soft and pliable.
Toss the onion with the vinegar to lightly pickle it, if not already pickled.
Fill the tortilla generously with the cheese, cream, and pickled onion, and roll up tightly, wrapping in a small bag for eating on the go.
Quesillo, a soft cheese wrapped in tortilla with cream and onion, is one of Nicaragua's most beloved street foods, especially associated with the town of La Paz Centro.

ingredients_full.note: oz, as a queso quesillo stand-in

steps_detailed: Warm the tortilla until soft and pliable.
Toss the onion with the vinegar to lightly pickle it, if not already pickled.
Fill the tortilla generously with the cheese, cream, and pickled onion, and roll up tightly, wrapping in a small bag for eating on the go.
Quesillo, a soft cheese wrapped in tortilla with cream and onion, is one of Nicaragua's most beloved street foods, especially associated with the town of La Paz Centro.
Sour cream is thinner and tangier than cream cheese, closer to the real consistency of Latin American crema or natilla, which pours rather than spreads.

name: Nicaraguan Repochetas

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour
Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Fry the tortilla until crisp.
Brown the beef, then layer with the refried beans and queso fresco onto the tortilla.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour
Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps_detailed: Fry the tortilla in a little oil until crisp and flat.
Brown the beef, then layer the refried beans, beef, queso fresco, and cabbage onto the crisp tortilla.
Repochetas, layered fried tortilla snacks, are a popular Nicaraguan lunch item.

name: Nicaraguan Sopa de Frijoles
steps: Simmer the beans with the onion and garlic in their liquid plus a splash of water for 15-18 minutes.
Blend partially for a thicker texture.
Top with cilantro and serve with tortillas.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and bell pepper until softened, then add the beans with their liquid and a splash of water.
Simmer 18-22 minutes, then blend partially for a thick, rustic texture.
Top with cilantro and queso fresco, and serve with warm tortillas - a simple, hearty Nicaraguan bean soup.

name: Nicaraguan Baho

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Layer the beef, plantain, cassava, and cabbage in a pot with a little water.
Cover tightly and steam-braise over low heat 1.5-2 hours until everything is very tender.
Serve together.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Layer the beef, plantain, cassava, cabbage, tomatoes, and vinegar in a heavy pot with a splash of water, covering tightly.

Cook over low heat 1.5-2.25 hours, checking occasionally, until the beef and vegetables are completely tender - this approximates baho's traditional method of steaming everything together, wrapped in plantain leaf.
Serve everything together on one plate - baho is widely considered Nicaragua's national dish, traditionally a Sunday family meal.

name: Nicaraguan Chancho con Yuca
steps: Marinate the pork with the garlic and lime juice for at least 20 minutes.
Roast or fry until deeply browned and cooked through.
Boil the cassava until tender and serve alongside.
steps_detailed: Marinate the pork with the garlic and lime juice for at least 25-30 minutes.
Fry or roast the pork in the oil until deeply browned and crisp outside, cooked through, 10-14 minutes.
Boil the cassava until tender, 14-18 minutes.
Serve the pork and cassava together with the shredded cabbage - chancho con yuca, fried pork with cassava, is a beloved Nicaraguan specialty.

name: Nicaraguan Ron Don
steps: Simmer the plantain and onion in the coconut milk for 10 minutes.
Add the fish and simmer 10-12 more minutes until cooked through.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Simmer the plantain, onion, and garlic in the coconut milk with a splash of water for 12-15 minutes.
Add the fish and simmer gently 10-14 more minutes until just cooked through and flaky.
Top with cilantro and serve with rice - ron don, a rich coconut seafood stew, reflects Nicaragua's Caribbean coast and Creole heritage.

name: Nicaraguan Vigoron
steps: Boil the cassava until tender, about 20 minutes.
Fry the pork until crisp.
Toss the cabbage with the lime juice and serve everything piled together on a plate.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava until fully tender, 20-25 minutes, then drain.
Fry the pork until deeply browned and crisp, 8-10 minutes.
Toss the cabbage with the lime juice, tomatoes, and jalapeno for a bright, crunchy slaw.
Serve the cassava, fried pork, and slaw piled together on a banana leaf or plate - vigoron is a beloved Nicaraguan street food, especially associated with the city of Granada.

name: Nicaraguan Nacatamales
steps: Whisk the masa harina with warm water into a thick dough.
Brown the pork with the potato.
Spread the dough on parchment or a soaked corn husk, top with the pork, potato, and plantain, fold into a packet, and steam 1 hour.
steps_detailed: Whisk the masa harina with warm water and the oil into a thick, smooth dough.
Brown the pork with the potato, 10-12 minutes.
Spread a layer of the masa dough onto parchment or a soaked plantain leaf, top with the pork, potato, plantain slices, and cilantro, then fold into a sealed packet.
Steam 1-1.25 hours until firm - nacatamales, larger and more elaborate than their neighbors' tamales, are a Nicaraguan weekend tradition.

name: Nicaraguan Cassava Chips
steps: Slice the cassava as thin as possible.
Fry in the oil until golden and crisp, in batches.
steps_detailed: Slice the cassava as thin and evenly as possible.
Fry in batches in the hot oil, 4-5 minutes per batch, until deeply golden and crisp.
Finish with a squeeze of lime - a popular, simple Nicaraguan snack.

name: Nicaraguan Tres Leches Cake
steps: Beat the eggs and sugar until pale, then fold in the flour to make a simple sponge batter.
Bake at 350F for 18-20 minutes until springy.
Poke holes in the cake and pour the milk over, letting it soak in.
steps_detailed: Beat the eggs and sugar together until pale and thickened, 4-5 minutes.
Fold in the flour and vanilla gently until just combined.
Bake in a small buttered dish at 350F (175C) for 18-22 minutes until springy and golden. Let cool slightly.
Poke holes all over the cake and pour the milk and coconut milk mixture over, letting it soak in fully.
Chill before serving - tres leches cake is beloved throughout Nicaragua and the wider region.

name: Nicaraguan Cajeta de Coco
steps: Simmer the coconut with the sugar and coconut milk for 8-10 minutes until thick and sticky.
Press into a small dish and chill before cutting into squares.
steps_detailed: Simmer the coconut with the sugar, coconut milk, and cinnamon over medium-low heat, stirring frequently, 10-12 minutes until thick and sticky.
Press firmly into a small lined dish and chill at least 1 hour until firm.
Cut into squares - cajeta de coco, a dense coconut candy, is a beloved Nicaraguan sweet.

name: Nicaraguan Tostones
steps: Fry the plantain slices until just golden, then flatten each with the back of a spoon.
Fry again until crisp on both sides.
Season with salt and serve.
steps_detailed: Fry the plantain slices in the oil over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until just golden but not fully crisp.
Remove and flatten each piece with the back of a spoon or a flat plate.
Fry the flattened pieces again, 2-3 minutes per side, until deeply golden and crisp.
Season with salt and a squeeze of lime - tostones, twice-fried green plantains, are a beloved crunchy Nicaraguan snack.

name: Nicaraguan Buñuelos de Yuca
steps: Mash the boiled cassava and mix with the crumbled queso fresco and egg into a soft dough.
Shape into small balls and fry until golden.
Drizzle with honey or syrup if you have it.
steps_detailed: Mash the boiled cassava until smooth and mix with the crumbled queso fresco and egg into a soft, cohesive dough.
Shape into small balls and fry in the oil over medium heat, turning, 4-6 minutes until deeply golden on all sides.
Drizzle with honey before serving - buñuelos de yuca are a beloved Nicaraguan holiday treat, especially at Christmas.

name: Nicaraguan Chimichurri Nicaragüense
steps: Combine the cabbage, onion, vinegar, and lime juice.
Let sit 15 minutes and serve over grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Combine the finely shredded cabbage, onion, vinegar, lime juice, and cilantro.
Season with salt and let sit at least 15-20 minutes so the vegetables lightly pickle - this bright, tangy relish tops grilled meats across Nicaragua.

name: Nicaraguan Cebollas Encurtidas
steps: Combine the onion, vinegar, and jalapeno.
Let sit at least 20 minutes to pickle before serving.
steps_detailed: Combine the onion, vinegar, jalapeno, and sugar.
Let sit at least 25-30 minutes so the onion softens and lightly pickles - pickled onions are the essential filling ingredient for quesillo and a common topping across Nicaraguan dishes.
